title: Coarse dynamics and partially hyperbolic diffeomorphisms in 3-manifolds
speaker: Rafael Potrie
affiliation: Universidad de la República, Uruguay; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics

proof_stages.label: Finite-time hyperbolicity and the 3-dimensional topological problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames partial hyperbolicity as a robust, finite-time/checkable condition that can constrain long-term dynamics, then restricts attention to closed 3-manifolds, especially hyperbolic ones, to ask which topologies and isotopy classes support such diffeomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Collapsed Anosov-flow target theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main result is presented in increasing strength: a hyperbolic 3-manifold with a partially hyperbolic diffeomorphism admits an Anosov flow, and in fact the diffeomorphism has a collapsed Anosov-flow structure described by an Anosov flow, a self orbit equivalence, and a continuous center-tangent collapse map.

proof_stages.label: Stable/unstable foliations and the center-integrability gap
proof_stages.summary: The stable and unstable one-dimensional bundles are uniquely integrated into invariant foliations by stable-manifold-type arguments, whereas the center direction and the center-stable/center-unstable sums need not integrate automatically, creating the main structural obstacle.

proof_stages.label: Toy non-integrability theorem on the unit tangent bundle
proof_stages.summary: As a simplified setting for the coarse-dynamics idea, the speaker states and sets up a contradiction proof that a partially hyperbolic diffeomorphism of the unit tangent bundle of a higher-genus surface inducing a pseudo-Anosov map on the base cannot admit an invariant center-stable foliation.

proof_stages.label: Horizontal foliation reduction and lifted product dynamics
proof_stages.summary: Assuming such a center-stable foliation exists, one proves it is horizontal, lifts it to the intermediate cover looking like a disk times a circle, and obtains a product-form description of deck transformations and of a lift of the diffeomorphism on the leaf/fiber coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Coarse pseudo-Anosov dynamics and the compact-set contradiction
proof_stages.summary: A suitable lift of the pseudo-Anosov base dynamics has attracting and repelling boundary behavior whose exponential large-scale effect beats bounded errors, producing a compact invariant set meeting every lifted leaf; this forces expansion of a circle homeomorphism, which is impossible.

proof_stages.label: Branching-foliation strategy in hyperbolic 3-manifolds
proof_stages.summary: For the full hyperbolic 3-manifold theorem, Burago-Ivanov branching foliations replace genuine center-stable/center-unstable foliations, a bounded-distance lift and a leaf-space dichotomy reduce non-fixed behavior to a translation case with a transverse pseudo-Anosov flow, and coarse dynamics plus fixed-point/index arguments lead toward the collapsed Anosov-flow structure.

examples.name: Geodesic flow on a negatively curved surface
examples.note: The geodesic flow is used as the historical and conceptual source of the move from finite geometric data, negative curvature, to long-term hyperbolic/ergodic dynamics, and later as a contrast showing why a compact coarse-dynamical set is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: the geodesic flow is described as the motivating example and its known ergodicity/time-one partial hyperbolicity is stated, but no concrete computation or verification is carried out on it

examples.name: Discretized Anosov flows
examples.note: Maps obtained by flowing each point for a positive time along an Anosov-flow orbit concretely illustrate the collapsed-Anosov-flow definition in the special case where the orbit equivalence fixes every orbit.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker concretely describes discretized Anosov flows by taking beta to fix each orbit and maps of the form x ↦ phi_{t(x)}(x), explaining what this construction yields

examples.name: Bonatti-Gogolev-Hammerlindl examples with non-homeomorphic collapse
examples.note: These examples show that the semiconjugating map h in the collapsed-Anosov-flow structure cannot in general be required to be a homeomorphism, thereby motivating the weaker collapsed formulation.
examples.engagement_reason: the Bonatti-Gogolev-Hammerlindl examples are invoked by existence to motivate why h need not be a homeomorphism, but their construction or the non-homeomorphic collapse is not worked out

passes.reason: the non-homeomorphic collapse is a genuinely collapsed semiconjugacy used to disprove the stronger homeomorphism expectation
